title: The Mail has questions to answer after its victory over Prince Harry
gist: Alan Rusbridger argues the Daily Mail's High Court win over Prince Harry was a technical victory on evidentiary grounds after 20+ years, not vindication—its collapsed star witness and £3m spent on private investigators still expose an intrusive tabloid culture.

insights.claim: The Daily Mail won its High Court case against Prince Harry and fellow claimants, with former editor Paul Dacre calling it 'an overwhelming vindication of our journalism'.
insights.speaker: Alan Rusbridger
insights.so_what: A landmark UK media-law outcome shaping the litigation risk facing tabloid publishers.
insights.locator: The Daily Mail, at its best

insights.claim: The claimants lost because after gaps of more than 20 years the evidence of illegality did not exist: in 57 instances the judge found strong suspicion but no proof, and their star private-investigator witness Gavin Burrows proved 'flaky or a fantasist'.
insights.speaker: Alan Rusbridger
insights.so_what: The win rested on evidentiary failure and ropey legal advice, not exoneration—limits its precedential weight.

insights.claim: The claimants established that Associated Newspapers paid an 'eye-watering' £3m to private investigators over the years, but with no clinching proof those investigators were tasked with illegal actions.
insights.speaker: Alan Rusbridger
insights.so_what: Quantifies tabloid investigative spend even where illegality couldn't be proven.

insights.claim: A Mail journalist devoted days to uncovering whether a woman in the public eye was pregnant—discovering it was an ectopic pregnancy, the doctor's and psychologist's names—in a draft article the judge said would have been 'grotesque and unjustifiable'.
insights.speaker: Alan Rusbridger
insights.so_what: Concrete illustration of intrusive practices that fuel reputational and legal risk for tabloids.
insights.locator: Mr Justice Nicklin’s War and Peace-length judgment

insights.claim: The Mail's gossip operation evolved from Nigel Dempster's single page into the online 'sidebar of shame', generating celebrity tittle-tattle 'every single minute of the day' as a highly lucrative business.
insights.speaker: Alan Rusbridger
insights.so_what: Celebrity-gossip content remains a core monetisation engine for the Mail's web model.
insights.locator: Once upon a time it penned off

insights.claim: Rupert Murdoch would rather pay out £1bn in costs and damages than risk a series of trials examining what happened at the Sun, and Mirror Group similarly wrote enormous cheques to avoid scrutiny under oath.
insights.speaker: Alan Rusbridger
insights.so_what: Reveals the scale publishers will pay to avoid discovery—a hidden liability on tabloid balance sheets.

insights.claim: Three forces changed Fleet Street: Nick Davies's reporting on criminality, the Leveson Inquiry, and lawsuits proving Murdoch and Mirror Group newsrooms had become 'lawless bear pits'.
insights.speaker: Alan Rusbridger
insights.so_what: Maps the accountability mechanisms that reshaped UK press-conduct risk.

insights.claim: Reporters from the Times, Guardian and Sunday Times digging into Nigel Farage's finances have uncovered material so murky that the Reform leader 'may well, in the long run, be finished'.
insights.speaker: Alan Rusbridger
insights.so_what: Flags investigative-press pressure as a live political risk to Farage and Reform.
